regulationSummary.overview: Connecticut has essentially no gold prospecting activity and no known productive placer gold deposits. The state is underlain by a mix of Precambrian metamorphic rocks, Triassic sedimentary basins (Connecticut River Valley), and glacial deposits. Connecticut is excellent for mineral collecting — the state has some of the finest mineral collecting sites in the northeastern US, including titanite (sphene), garnet, and tourmaline localities — but gold prospecting is not a viable recreational activity.
keyRules: Connecticut does not have a specific recreational gold prospecting permit system — there are no productive gold deposits in the state.
In-stream work in Connecticut waterways is regulated by the Connecticut Department of Energy and Environmental Protection (DEEP) under stream channel encroachment and wetland statutes.
Connecticut state forests allow mineral collecting for personal use — contact the relevant state forest office for current rules.
No collecting is permitted in Connecticut state parks.
regulationSummary.hpaInfo: Not applicable — there are no gold-bearing streams in Connecticut suitable for prospecting.
regulationSummary.suctionDredgeStatus: Not applicable — Connecticut has no productive placer gold streams.
regulationSummary.handPanning: Gold panning in Connecticut streams is not a viable activity — the state has no known placer gold deposits. Connecticut mineral collectors should focus on the state's excellent garnet, tourmaline, and titanite localities in state forest areas.
regulationSummary.disclaimer: Regulations change. Verify current rules at the official sources listed below before going out.

officialResources.title: Connecticut Geological Survey
officialResources.description: State geological survey — Connecticut has no gold-bearing geology but excellent mineral collecting opportunities in metamorphic terranes.

officialResources.title: CT DEEP — State Forests and Parks
officialResources.description: Connecticut DEEP manages state forests where personal mineral collecting is generally allowed — contact the specific forest for current rules.
